name: Mail-In Ballot Fraud
description: Mail-in ballot fraud is a conspiracy theory alleging widespread fraudulent activity related to voting by mail in United States elections. Proponents claim that mail-in ballots are susceptible to various forms of manipulation, including ballot harvesting, double voting, and the casting of ballots in the names of deceased individuals or those who have moved. These claims often suggest that such fraud is rampant and capable of significantly altering election outcomes. The theory gained significant traction during and after the 2020 presidential election. Evidence cited by proponents often includes anecdotal reports of irregularities, statistical anomalies in election results, and selective interpretations of voter registration data. Mainstream perspectives from election officials, legal experts, and academic researchers consistently refute claims of widespread mail-in ballot fraud. They point to the numerous safeguards in place to prevent and detect fraud, such as signature verification, ballot tracking, and post-election audits. Studies have repeatedly shown that voter fraud, including mail-in ballot fraud, is extremely rare. The theory is often fueled by partisan motivations and distrust in the electoral process, and it has contributed to increased political polarization and challenges to election legitimacy. The Brennan Center for Justice and other non-partisan organizations have published extensive reports debunking claims of widespread mail-in ballot fraud and highlighting the security measures in place. Despite the lack of credible evidence, the theory persists and continues to influence political discourse and policy debates surrounding voting rights and election administration.
subtitle: Allegations of widespread election manipulation.

claims.claim: Mail-in ballots are easily susceptible to fraud.
claims.evidence: Anecdotal reports of irregularities, claims of lax security protocols.
claims.criticism: Studies consistently show voter fraud is rare; security measures like signature verification and ballot tracking exist.

claims.claim: Dead people vote via mail-in ballots.
claims.evidence: Reports of deceased individuals appearing on voter rolls, statistical anomalies.
claims.criticism: Voter rolls are imperfect but cleaned regularly; double-checking occurs. Few proven cases of actual fraudulent voting.

claims.claim: Ballot harvesting leads to widespread fraud.
claims.evidence: Concerns about third parties collecting and submitting ballots.
claims.criticism: Ballot harvesting is legal in some states with restrictions; evidence of widespread fraud is lacking. Laws regulate who can handle ballots.

keyFigures.name: Donald Trump
keyFigures.role: Promoted the theory of widespread mail-in ballot fraud during and after the 2020 election.

keyFigures.name: Hans von Spakovsky
keyFigures.role: Senior Legal Fellow at the Heritage Foundation; promotes claims of voter fraud.

keyFigures.name: Myrna Pérez
keyFigures.role: Director of the Voting Rights and Elections Program at the Brennan Center for Justice; debunks fraud claims.

mythologyConnections: The concept of a rigged game or contest.

timeline.event: Concerns about absentee ballot fraud emerge.
Mail-in voting increases due to the COVID-19 pandemic, leading to heightened claims of fraud.
Numerous lawsuits and audits challenge election results based on claims of mail-in ballot fraud, largely unsuccessful.

sources.author: Peralta, Elizabeth
sources.work: Fact check: Trump makes multiple false claims about mail-in voting
sources.title: Peralta, Elizabeth
